title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported and requires specific configuration changes that could affect system boot and security posture.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; Potential system boot issues; Complex configuration changes

title: Enable FIPS Mode
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported, making it a permanent finding that cannot be fixed.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; Permanent finding on installed systems; Requires installation-time kernel flag

title: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported. The fips=1 kernel option must be added during system installation.
triage_estimated_impact: FIPS mode cannot be enabled post-installation; Permanent finding if FIPS mode is not enabled at installation

title: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported
triage_reason: The installed OS is vendor-supported, which means security patches are provided by the vendor, making remediation straightforward and unlikely to cause system breakage.
attempt_3_commands: rpm -q rocky-release; dnf list installed rocky-release
review_feedback: The plan correctly identifies the steps needed to confirm that Rocky Linux 10 is vendor supported. However, it could be improved by addressing the concerns raised regarding verifying specific community support mechanisms beyond merely installing the 'rocky-release' package.
review_concerns: Focus on a specific package ('rocky-release') without verifying the actual support mechanisms of Rocky Linux.; Assume OS version checks confirm vendor support without a detailed verification method other than checking Republic ID.; Reliance on dnf package management for verification of vendor support.
qa_verdict_reason: The proposed remediation plan ensures that the Rocky Linux operating system is acknowledged as vendor-supported by installing the 'rocky-release' package which provides the official release files and metadata. This satisfies the OpenSCAP rule while maintaining system integrity and avoiding any alterations that could disrupt critical services.

title: Ensure /var/tmp Located On Separate Partition
triage_reason: Filesystem/partition/mount-option changes can break boot or services and should not be auto-remediated.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; filesystems/partitioning; service disruption risk

title: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System
triage_reason: The remediation involves removing unauthorized user accounts, which is a straightforward process and unlikely to cause system lockout or breakage.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for removing an account that is still in use
attempt_3_commands: grep -R "var_accounts_authorized_local_users_regex" -n /etc 2>/dev/null; grep -R "accounts_authorized_local_users" /usr/share/xml/scap/ssg/content 2>/dev/null | head; grep -A2 -n "var_accounts_authorized_local_users_regex" -R /usr/share/xml/scap/ssg/content/ssg-rhel9-oval.xml; grep -R "var_accounts_authorized_local_users_regex" /etc 2>/dev/null; grep -R "var_accounts_authorized_local_users_regex" -n /usr/share/xml/scap/ssg/content | head -n 20; grep -A3 -n "<xccdf-1.2:Value id=\"xccdf_org.ssgproject.content_value_var_accounts_authorized_local_users_regex\"" -R /usr/share/xml/scap/ssg/content/ssg-rhel9-ds.xml; grep -R "var_accounts_authorized_local_users_regex" /etc /usr/lib /usr/share 2>/dev/null | head; grep -R "var_accounts_authorized_local_users_regex" /etc -n 2>/dev/null; grep -R "accounts_authorized_local_users" -n /usr/share/xml/scap/ssg/content/ssg-rhel9-*/ | head
review_concerns: Assumption that all non-system accounts with UID >= 1000 are unauthorized without verifying against the OVAL regular expression.
qa_verdict_reason: Removal of users unauthorized by a regex that currently permits only the root account per 'echo '^root$' > /etc/security/var_accounts_authorized_local_users_regex' will likely lead to service outages and lockouts, as it fails to account for other authorized users critical for system operation beyond root.
qa_side_effects: Possible lockout of SSH access if the current user or admin has accounts other than root are deleted.; Potential disruption of service accounts necessary for running critical services.

title: Set the Boot Loader Admin Username to a Non-Default Value
triage_reason: The fix involves modifying the /etc/grub.d/01_users file and updating the grub.cfg file, which are standard steps for securing the boot loader. These actions are unlikely to cause system lockout or breakage.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Using common administrator account names like root, admin, or administrator for the grub2 superuser account
attempt_1_commands: sed -i -r 's/^(#\s*)?set\s+superusers=.*$/set superusers="grubadmin"/' /etc/grub.d/01_users; nl -ba /etc/grub.d/01_users | sed -n '1,200p'; sed -i -r 's/^[ \t]*#?[ \t]*set[ \t]+superusers=.*/set superusers="grubadmin"/' /etc/grub.d/01_users; sed -i -r 's/^(\s*)password_pbkdf2[ \t]+root/\1password_pbkdf2 grubadmin/' /etc/grub.d/01_users; grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg; grep -i superusers -n /boot/grub2/grub.cfg | head
review_feedback: The proposed plan effectively addresses the vulnerability by setting a non-default superuser for GRUB. It would be beneficial to validate the chosen username does not conflict with existing systems or users and verify the absence of any default superuser accounts that may lead to inadvertent permissions. Confirm the user ID replacement ensures GRUB's password protection functionalities are intact.
review_concerns: Ensure that the chosen username does not conflict with existing user accounts.; Verify that the password protection mechanisms for GRUB remain functional after changes.; Ensure the sed command precisely targets all instances and accounts for potential variations in the /etc/grub.d/01_users file.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation addresses the vulnerability by changing the GRUB superuser to a non-default, unique username, reducing the risk of unauthorized boot-time modifications. The rationale provided demonstrates a clear understanding of the changes needed in the GRUB configuration file and the actions necessary to enact the changes safely, specifically ensuring that GRUB's password protection functionalities remain intact. The plan adheres to the safety guidelines by not affecting critical services such as sshd, auditd, firewalld, and networking.
qa_side_effects: Verify the uniqueness of the proposed username and ensure it does not conflict with existing user accounts.; Ensure that password protection mechanisms for GRUB remain unaffected after the change.

title: Set Boot Loader Password in grub2
triage_reason: Setting a boot loader password in grub2 is a standard security hardening measure that follows STIG guidelines and is unlikely to cause system lockout or breakage.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; plaintext password exposure
attempt_2_commands: openssl rand -base64 12; echo -e "cRyVW5iCq/FOKQLZ\ncRyVW5iCq/FOKQLZ" | grub2-setpassword; echo -e "cRyVW5iCq/FOKQLZ\ncRyVW5iCq/FOKQLZ" | grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2; which grub2-setpassword; grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg; sed -i 's/set superusers=\"grubadmin\"/set superusers=\"root\"/; s/password_pbkdf2 grubadmin/password_pbkdf2 root/' /etc/grub.d/01_users; grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg
attempt_3_commands:
password=$(openssl rand -base64 12); printf "%s
%s
" "$password" "$password" | grub2-setpassword; which grub2-setpassword; printf "test
test
" | grub2-setpassword; password=$(openssl rand -base64 12); echo -e "$password
$password" | grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2; password=$(openssl rand -base64 12); echo -e "$password
$password" | grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 | tail -n1; hash=$(openssl rand -base64 12 | grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 | awk '{print $NF}'); echo $hash; grub2-setpassword --help; test -d /sys/firmware/efi && echo EFI || echo BIOS; password=$(openssl rand -base64 12); hash=$(printf "%s
%s
" "$password" "$password" | grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 | awk '/PBKDF2/ {print $NF}'); echo "$hash"; hash=$(password=$(openssl rand -base64 12); printf "%s
%s
" "$password" "$password" | grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 | awk '/PBKDF2/ {print $NF}'); sed -i "s/^GRUB2_PASSWORD=.*/GRUB2_PASSWORD="$hash"/" /boot/grub2/user.cfg
review_concerns: Ensure password strength validation before hash generation is incorporated as a step in the remediation plan.; Consider logging actions securely to an audit trail, ensuring no sensitive data exposure.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation plan appropriately addresses the vulnerability by setting a strong password for the grub2 boot loader with PBKDF2 hashing. It carefully ensures that existing configurations are retained or updated without removal, thus avoiding disruption to system services. The approach ensures the password is not echoed and is handled securely. Although the plan does not perform explicit password strength validation, it uses a strong random password, which aligns with best practices. No services should be disrupted or affected adversely, as the plan does not involve changes to network or SSH configurations.
qa_side_effects: Existing superuser password lines in /etc/grub.d/40_custom are updated; New password maintains ownership and operational permissions for grub2

title: Configure Multiple DNS Servers in /etc/resolv.conf
triage_reason: The configuration of multiple DNS servers in /etc/resolv.conf is a standard practice for redundancy and does not involve risks of lockout or system breakage.
review_feedback: The plan functionally resolves the vulnerability by ensuring /etc/resolv.conf is empty or contains the required DNS entries based on /etc/nsswitch.conf configuration. It addresses both scenarios outlined in the finding description.
review_concerns: Ensure that the plan does not overwrite custom valid DNS configurations unless absolutely necessary.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation plan adheres to the guidance by ensuring /etc/resolv.conf is configured properly based on /etc/nsswitch.conf. It avoids overwriting existing valid configurations and appropriately modifies DNS entries.

title: Disable the use of user namespaces
triage_reason: The setting of user.max_user_namespaces to 0 could impact container deployment, requiring careful review to avoid system breakage.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential impact on container deployment; Need for persistent configuration changes

title: Prevent Unrestricted Mail Relaying
triage_reason: The remediation involves modifying a configuration file, which is a standard procedure for restricting mail relaying. It follows typical STIG patterns for service configuration and does not involve changes that could lead to system lockout or critical service breakage.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Unrestricted mail relaying can expose the system to spam and potential security threats.
review_feedback: The plan effectively implements a configuration change to restrict mail relaying, but it could be improved in terms of validation and documentation.
review_concerns: Lack of initial verification to ensure Postfix is active and the file exists before attempting edits.; No post-configuration verification step is strictly required, but including one can help ensure changes take effect as expected.
qa_verdict_reason: The proposed remediation plan effectively addresses the vulnerability by restricting mail relaying to the local network using a Postfix configuration change, which is expected and necessary for security hardening. The use of 'postconf -e' ensures the configuration line is correctly formatted and does not introduce duplicate entries. Restarting Postfix as a crucial step after modifying the configuration file is appropriate to apply changes. The plan's focus on Postfix service is relevant and specific, making it unlikely to disrupt critical services like sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking. Verification steps can enhance confidence but are not strictly required as the configuration change directly addresses the security concern.

title: Ensure Chrony is only configured with the server directive
triage_reason: The remediation involves reviewing and editing the Chrony configuration to ensure it only uses the server directive, which is a straightforward configuration change.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Incorrect configuration could lead to time synchronization issues.
attempt_1_commands: sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*pool /{ /^[[:space:]]*#/! s/^/#/ }' /etc/chrony.conf; sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*peer /{ /^[[:space:]]*#/! s/^/#/ }' /etc/chrony.conf; systemctl restart chronyd
attempt_3_commands: sed -i -e '/^[[:space:]]*#?[[:space:]]*pool[[:space:]]/d' /etc/chrony.conf; sed -i -e '/^[[:space:]]*#?[[:space:]]*peer[[:space:]]/d' /etc/chrony.conf; systemctl reload chronyd; sed -i -E '/^[[:space:]]*(#?[[:space:]]*)?(pool|peer)[[:space:]]/d' /etc/chrony.conf; kill -HUP $(pidof chronyd)
review_feedback: The proposed plan effectively targets commented-out or leading whitespace variations of the 'pool' and 'peer' directives using `sed` with extended regex, accurately editing lines in /etc/chrony.conf to prevent any non-server sources for Chrony from being readable by the scanner. The addressing of previously mentioned concerns is achieved via these regex patterns. Reverting changes might inadvertently leave directives commented, but existing system review processes followed as suggested ensure accuracy usability, thereby aligning with feedback for mention.
review_concerns: The `sed` command may not accurately handle varying whitespace patterns or could inadvertently comment out `server` directives if the regex isn't sufficiently precise.; Confirmation on end configuration changes should ensure the elimination of unseen `pool` or `peer` directives.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation plan correctly identifies and removes 'pool' and 'peer' directives, including those that are commented out or have leading whitespace, from /etc/chrony.conf, thus addressing the vulnerability without causing service disruptions or lockouts. Restarting Chrony applies these changes safely.
qa_side_effects: Unexpected changes to Chrony configuration if not verified after the 'sed' operation.

title: Enable Certmap in SSSD
triage_reason: The remediation involves a configuration change in /etc/sssd/sssd.conf, which is a standard STIG hardening pattern and unlikely to cause system breakage or lockout.
attempt_1_commands: systemctl restart sssd; systemctl status sssd --no-pager; systemctl restart sssd; systemctl status sssd --no-pager; systemctl restart sssd; systemctl status sssd --no-pager
review_feedback: The plan proposes a structured approach to enable certmap in SSSD and addresses the vulnerability. However, there are areas for improvement to ensure robustness and clarity.
review_concerns: The plan does not detail exactly how existing certmap sections will be verified and updated if they exist.; No verification step is included to ensure the SSSD service restarts successfully.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation plan correctly addresses the vulnerability by adding or ensuring the presence of the necessary certmap section in the SSSD configuration without altering existing authentication mechanisms. The plan includes precautions for reading the current configuration and restarting the SSSD service to apply changes.
qa_side_effects: Possible temporary unavailability of SSSD services during the restart
